subject: civic
learning_objectives: Identify the signs and symptoms of AIDS
Explain what is meant by stigmatisation of HIV/AIDS patients

sections.heading: Meaning of HIV/AIDS
sections.content:
HIV means Human Immunodeficiency Virus. AIDS is Acquired Immune Deficiency Syndrome.

AIDS is a disease of the human immune system caused by the human immunodeficiency virus. AIDS is a virus that weakens or destroys the immune system of the body, leaving it prey to many infections that would otherwise be easily suppressed. The immunity is the ability of the body to resist an infection. When the immune system of the body is destroyed by this virus, the person can easily catch every disease around him or her, and the body will not resist any infection. AIDS is also described as a slim sickness.

HIV is transmitted through direct contact of the bloodstream with a bodily fluid containing HIV. Such bodily fluids include semen, vaginal fluid, and breast milk. There is no cure for this disease; preventing infection is the best way to reduce its widespread impact.

sections.heading: Causes of HIV/AIDS
sections.content:
HIV/AIDS is transmitted from one person to another through any of the following ways:

a. Sexual Transmission: The majority of HIV infections are acquired through unprotected sexual relations between two partners, one of whom has HIV. When a person has many sexual partners, the chances of contracting HIV are very high. During a sexual act, only male or female condoms can reduce the risk of infection with HIV.

b. Blood Transfusion: The transfusion of infected blood (unscreened blood) causes HIV and other sexually transmitted diseases (STDs) such as gonorrhoea, herpes, syphilis, chlamydia, candidiasis, and trichomoniasis.

c. Mother-to-Child Transmission: HIV-infected mothers can infect their infants at or before birth. Transmission can also occur through breast milk. When the baby sucks the breasts of an infected mother, he or she contacts the virus. HIV-infected mothers should avoid breastfeeding their infants.

d. Contaminated Sharp Objects: The use of contaminated sharp objects such as razors, scalpels, glass, blades, syringes, and barber clippers infected by the virus can cause HIV. However, when these sharp objects are properly sterilised, the infection is reduced.

HIV and AIDS cannot be spread through the following ways:
i. By handshake
ii. By hugging
iii. By sharing clothes
iv. By eating together with an infected person
v. By living in the same residence or house with an infected person

sections.heading: Signs and Symptoms of AIDS
sections.content:
People with AIDS experience the following signs and symptoms:

a. Prolonged Cough: The AIDS patient coughs continuously for a very long period of time. The cough does not stop no matter the drug taken.

b. Prolonged Diarrhoea: The stool of the patient is watery and comes out very often.

c. Prolonged Fever and Malaria: The body temperature is usually very high. The patient is always diagnosed with malaria, and even when malaria drugs are given, the malaria is never cured.

d. Rashes: There are lots of red spots on the body, including on the tongue.

e. Swollen Glands: A gland is an organ in the body that produces a substance the body needs, such as hormones, sweat, or saliva. When the glands are swollen, they cannot function very well.

f. Weight Loss and Weakness: The patient's weight continues to decrease without explanation, and the patient is always weak and unable to do things he could do before.

g. Loss of Appetite: The patient will not have appetite for food. When food is provided, he hardly eats.

h. Much Sweating: The patient sweats regularly, especially at night.

sections.heading: Effects of HIV/AIDS
sections.content:
a. Death: When the infection is prolonged, death can occur. In most cases, children end up losing both parents and become orphans.

b. Depression: The HIV/AIDS patient becomes depressed while the family goes through emotional and psychological torture.

c. Destruction of Immune System: The immune system of the patient is destroyed. The body cannot protect itself against diseases, and the patient suffers from every disease available.

d. Manpower Loss: When a person has a full-blown case of AIDS, he is no longer productive. This affects the total number of people working in the country.

e. Psychological Effects: As there is no cure for AIDS, no matter how well the parents and relations try to care for the person, death must surely occur at a certain time. There will be mental torture, anger, frustration, and emotional depression in the parents, brothers, sisters, and relations of the sufferer.

f. Financial Burden: The parents and relations shouldering the financial responsibility of caring for the HIV/AIDS patient may not be able to send other members of the family to school. The entire family is badly affected economically.

sections.heading: Preventive Measures of HIV/AIDS
sections.content:
a. Abstinence: People should avoid having sex before marriage. When they get married, they must remain faithful to their marriage partners. However, if people cannot abstain from sex, they should use a latex condom.

b. Avoid Sharing Sharp Objects: People should stop sharing needles, syringes, razors, clippers, toothbrushes, manicure sets, sharp combs, and chewing sticks.

c. Blood Screening: Blood transfusion must be approached with caution. If it is necessary that blood be given to a patient, the blood must be screened in a reputable laboratory.

d. Enlightenment: Government, non-governmental organisations, civil societies, and religious groups should organise seminars, workshops, and conferences to enlighten people on the dangers of this killer disease.

e. HIV Test Certificate: Religious bodies should demand an HIV test certificate before couples can be joined together as husband and wife. Intending couples should also go for HIV tests on their own to know their status.

sections.heading: Stigmatisation of HIV/AIDS Patients
sections.content:
AIDS patients are treated as outcasts. They are stigmatised and avoided by even their friends. The patients feel ashamed and avoid appearing in public so as not to be ridiculed, humiliated, and discriminated against.

The stigmatisation of the AIDS patient is due to a number of factors:

a. Ignorance: Most people do not know the causes of HIV/AIDS. Even when infected persons are near them, they avoid them out of fear and misinformation.

b. Punishment from God: In some societies, AIDS patients are believed to have been punished by God because of their evil deeds. This notion is wrong and should be criticised.

c. Promiscuity: Some people believe that AIDS patients had many sexual relationships with others, hence the disease. They fail to know that the disease can be caused by other factors apart from sexual contact.
